uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Heading level jumps from H1 to H3 — skipping 1 level(s). Heading text: "On-Air Anime"
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers use heading structure to let users navigate the page by section. Skipping levels confuses this outline and breaks WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.3.1. Search engines also use heading hierarchy to understand content structure, so gaps can reduce SEO ranking.
uiIssues.recommendation: Change the H3 to an H2 so the outline is sequential. If you need visual size variation, use CSS classes to style headings rather than skipping levels for presentational purposes.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Image 4 (src: "https://i0.wp.com/animewatchtv.su/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/hell-mode-yarikomiz") has no alt attribute or an empty one.
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers announce this image as "image" with no context, making it meaningless to visually-impaired users. Search engines also use alt text to index images, so missing alt text reduces SEO value.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a descriptive alt attribute that conveys the purpose or content of the image. For purely decorative images use alt="" (empty string) to tell screen readers to skip it. Avoid generic text like "image" or "photo".

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Image 4 (src: "https://i2.wp.com/animewatchtv.su/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/jigokuraku-2nd-seas") has no alt attribute or an empty one.
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers announce this image as "image" with no context, making it meaningless to visually-impaired users. Search engines also use alt text to index images, so missing alt text reduces SEO value.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a descriptive alt attribute that conveys the purpose or content of the image. For purely decorative images use alt="" (empty string) to tell screen readers to skip it. Avoid generic text like "image" or "photo".

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: This page has no <meta name="description"> tag (or the content is empty).
uiIssues.impact: Search engines often display the meta description as the snippet text in search results. Without it, Google generates its own excerpt from page content, which may be irrelevant or unattractive to users — reducing click-through rate.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add <meta name="description" content="..."> inside <head> with a concise, keyword-rich summary of the page (ideally 150–160 characters). Each page should have a unique description.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Image 3 (src: "https://i3.wp.com/animewatchtv.su/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/high-school-kimengu") has no alt attribute or an empty one.

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

uiIssues.description: Image 3 (src: "https://i3.wp.com/animewatchtv.su/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/himitsu-no-aipri-ri") has no alt attribute or an empty one.
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers announce this image as "image" with no context, making it meaningless to visually-impaired users. Search engines also use alt text to index images, so missing alt text reduces SEO value.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a descriptive alt attribute that conveys the purpose or content of the image. For purely decorative images use alt="" (empty string) to tell screen readers to skip it. Avoid generic text like "image" or "photo".

uiIssues.description: Input field 1 (type="text") has no associated <label>, aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title attribute (has placeholder "Search..." — placeholder is not a substitute for a label).
uiIssues.impact: Screen readers cannot tell users what to type into this field. This is a WCAG 2.1 Level A violation (Success Criterion 1.3.1 and 3.3.2). Unlabelled fields are also harder to tap accurately on mobile because the touch target for the label is missing.
uiIssues.recommendation: Add a <label for="inputId"> element whose for attribute matches the input's id. If a visible label is not desired, use aria-label="Field purpose" on the input itself, or aria-labelledby pointing to an existing descriptive element. Note: placeholder text is not a valid replacement for a label.

uiIssues.description: Two interactive elements genuinely overlap: input and button. Intersection area: ~1020px² (94% of the smaller element). Detected at 1280×800 resolution.
uiIssues.impact: When clickable elements overlap, users may accidentally trigger the wrong action. On touchscreens the problem is amplified because touch targets are imprecise. This also breaks keyboard tab order, causing confusion for keyboard-only users.
uiIssues.recommendation: Use browser DevTools to identify the overlapping elements and fix their positioning (check for z-index stacking, absolute/fixed positioning, or negative margins). Ensure all interactive elements have a minimum touch target size of 44×44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5 Level AAA recommendation).

formIssues.description: Text input "#s" in form "#form" accepted the SQL injection string "' OR '1'='1" without any client-side sanitization or rejection.
formIssues.impact: If this input is passed unsanitised to a database query, an attacker can manipulate the query to read, modify, or delete arbitrary data — including user credentials and private records. SQL injection is consistently ranked #1 in the OWASP Top 10 web vulnerabilities.
formIssues.recommendation: Never construct SQL queries by concatenating user input. Use parameterised queries or prepared statements in all server-side database calls. On the client side, validate and reject obviously malicious patterns. Consider a Web Application Firewall (WAF) as an additional layer.

formIssues.description: Text input "#s" in form "#form" accepted a cross-site scripting (XSS) payload ("<script>alert("xss")</script>…") without sanitization.
formIssues.impact: If this input is reflected back in the page HTML without escaping, an attacker can inject scripts that steal session cookies, redirect users to malicious sites, or perform actions on behalf of the victim. Stored XSS can affect every subsequent visitor who views the content.
formIssues.recommendation: Escape all user-supplied content before rendering it as HTML (use textContent instead of innerHTML, or a templating library that auto-escapes). Apply a strict Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header. Consider a sanitisation library like DOMPurify for any HTML you must render dynamically.

formIssues.description: Form "form:nth-of-type(2)" has 467 input fields but none are marked as required.
formIssues.impact: Without required-field marking, users have no visual indication of which fields must be filled. This increases form abandonment and the likelihood of incomplete submissions reaching the server.
formIssues.recommendation: Add the required attribute to all mandatory inputs. Additionally, visually indicate required fields with an asterisk (*) and a legend explaining the symbol (e.g., "* Required field").
